Engineer who designs, builds, and validates — then iterates.

My background is hands-on mechanical engineering: I've cut, welded, and machined parts; built electromechanical test benches; and validated models against real data. At UPenn I'm combining that with physics-informed ML and robotics hardware. I care about decisions you can justify — not just outputs that look reasonable.

About

The way I work

I start with the physics or the constraint, build from first principles, then validate against something real — FEA, experimental data, or physical testing. That loop (design → analyze → build → test → iterate) came from years of BAJA fabrication, shop-floor CNC exposure, and research where "it looks right" isn't enough. Now I'm applying it to robotics hardware and physics-driven ML.

Selected Projects

Projects

Each project follows the same structure: context → my role → what I did, how, and why I made those decisions. Click any image to enlarge.

01 — Robotics Hardware
Coaxial UAV Motor Mount (DMLS Aluminum)
Vijay Kumar Lab, UPenn · Nov 2025 – Present
DMLSDfAM Load-path analysisGD&T drawingsTolerance stack-up
Context
The lab needed a motor-mount adapter to convert an existing UAV airframe to a coaxial rotor configuration. No off-the-shelf part existed; it had to be metal AM to hit weight and geometry constraints.
My Role
Full ownership from load-case definition through manufacturing-ready CAD and 2D drawings. Coordinated with controls and electrical teams on interface constraints.
What I Did & Why
  • Defined load cases first: mapped thrust, torque, and vibration loads before touching CAD — every geometric decision has a structural justification.
  • Chose DMLS over machining: the internal relief geometry required undercuts not achievable in a single fixturing setup — AM enabled load-path-optimized cutouts without fixturing constraints.
  • Tolerance stack-up: specified fits so alignment survives assembly variation — documented in GD&T drawings for repeatable builds.
  • CAD → part fidelity confirmed: the cutout pattern in the fabricated part matches the SolidWorks model exactly — drawings were correct.
02 — Fabrication & Validation
PSI Racing BAJA: Suspension & Steering (CAD → Build → Race)
NIT Tiruchirappalli · Aug 2021 – Jul 2024
Manual fabricationANSYS MATLABGD&TDAQ testing
Context
Subsystem head for SAE BAJA competition vehicle — responsible for suspension and steering from concept through physical vehicle testing. Three years, multiple design iterations, one built and raced car.
My Role
Full design-to-build-to-test loop: concept → CAD → GD&T drawings → machining coordination → welding & assembly → DAQ-instrumented vehicle testing → root-cause iteration.
What I Did & Why
  • FEA before cutting metal: validated in ANSYS under static/dynamic load cases; FoS > 1.8 maintained. Stress contours directly shaped where material was removed.
  • 12% mass reduction on steering knuckle — the curved cutout slots visible in the fabricated photo are placed exactly where FoS had the most margin.
  • Built it myself: cutting, welding, grinding, fit-up — so I understand how tolerances behave in real fabrication, not just simulation.
  • Vehicle-level DAQ testing: instrumented the car, validated against simulation; failures drove the next design iteration with documented root-cause analysis.
03 — Instrumentation
Planetary MR Clutch + Custom Test Bench
University of Western Ontario · May – Aug 2023
Electromechanical designTest bench build Arduino / DAQTorque sensing
Context
Research internship: design and validate a miniature planetary MR clutch for compliant, controllable torque transmission. The exploded CAD shows all components — clutch housing, coil, brass plate, ring/sun/planetary gears, roller bearing, rotary seal, input shaft.
My Role
Designed the clutch, built the test bench from scratch, developed the DAQ system, ran structured experiments, and performed root-cause analysis on performance bottlenecks.
What I Did & Why
  • Planetary geometry for shear area: MR fluid torque is shear-area-limited — geometry directly drives performance, so I maximized shear surface within the compact envelope.
  • Built the test bench myself: torque sensors, RPM sensing, current drivers, Arduino DAQ — off-the-shelf rigs couldn't measure at the required resolution.
  • Structured debugging: isolated variables systematically to pinpoint why output was below spec; iterated to reach 1.48 Nm.
  • MATLAB-automated post-processing correlated electrical input to mechanical output — made validation repeatable and removed manual error.
04 — Additive Manufacturing / FEA
WAAM Process Optimization (Simufact)
NIT Tiruchirappalli · Feb – Apr 2024
SimufactThermo-mechanical FEA Residual stressDistortion
Context
Final-year project: use thermo-mechanical FEA to understand and reduce residual stress and distortion in multi-layer Wire Arc AM builds. The image shows the 11 filler layers color-coded on the substrate plate — the visible wall warping is exactly the distortion this project targeted.
My Role
Built and ran all FEA models in Simufact, designed the parametric study, and coordinated physical validation against fabricated samples.
What I Did & Why
  • Mesh refinement strategy: refined elements in the HAZ and inter-layer interfaces where thermal gradients are steepest — coarser mesh elsewhere to keep run-time tractable.
  • Alternating scan direction hypothesis: predicted this would reduce cumulative thermal asymmetry; FEA confirmed it, guiding which parameters to physically test.
  • ~18% residual stress, ~22% distortion reduction — validated against physical samples to confirm the simulation was predictive, not just fitting noise.
05 — AI for Science
Physics-Informed Modeling: LMD Thermal PDEs
PyTorch · UPenn · 2024
PINNsHeat transfer Moving heat sourceAdaptive loss weights
Context
Traditional FEA for laser metal deposition is accurate but slow. Goal: train a PINN on the governing PDE so the network generalizes without labeled simulation data. Top row: 3-view temperature fields. Middle: melt pool profiles vs liquidus. Bottom: training loss, peak temperature evolution, adaptive loss weights.
My Role
Sole developer — PDE formulation, network architecture, training pipeline, and validation against Abaqus FEA and experimental benchmarks.
What I Did & Why
  • Goldak double-ellipsoid heat source: point-source approximations fail to capture melt-pool geometry in LMD — volumetric model is physically necessary.
  • Temperature-dependent k(T), Cp(T): constant properties produced >25% error in peak temperature — including them in the PDE residual was non-negotiable.
  • Adaptive loss weighting (PDE/IC/BC/HS): weights auto-balance shown in the bottom-right panel — stabilized training that otherwise diverged.
↗ Code (GitHub)
06 — AI for Science
PirateNet: Stable Physics-Informed Thermal Modeling for AM
JAX · UPenn · 2025
JAXMultiplicative gating Causal trainingNIST AM-Bench validation
Context
Standard MLPs failed on the AM thermal problem — gradients exploded and the network couldn't resolve the sharp Gaussian heat source. This project implemented PirateNet and systematically investigated what works and what doesn't — including honest reporting of failures.
My Role
Full pipeline in JAX: architecture, adaptive loss weighting, causal training scheduler, and a 3-method collocation sampling study.
What I Did & Why
  • Multiplicative gating: top-left plot shows MLP (blue) erratic; PirateNet (red) converges smoothly on both total loss and PDE residual.
  • Causal temporal training: earlier timesteps must converge before later ones — physically motivated; non-causal training produces acausal temperature transients.
  • Fourier features failed: they over-smooth the localized heat source — documented so future work doesn't repeat the mistake.
  • RAR sampling: no benefit at higher cost — sampling study (bottom-right) shows nearly identical temperature fields across all three methods. Reported honestly.
  • Validated vs NIST AM-Bench LPBF: ~11% relative error on melt-pool depth in conduction-mode regimes.
↗ Code (GitHub)
